text:
Summarize (Orchestrating the Discussion)

Solutions and Strategies

Have students discuss the rate tables.

Suggested Questions

If students do not explicitly mention the role of the unit rate, push them to think about it with these questions. (Language)

  • Which tables were easier for you to complete, the tables in Problem 3.1 or these?
  • How can the unit rate help you find equivalent ratios in the table?

• Most likely students will think that the patterns in this problem were easier. Students should mention using multiples of 4 or adding 4 more cups for each ounce of popcorn kernels.
  • If you know how much it is for 1 unit of something, it is easy to multiply by the greater quantity to find the price or amount

Ask students to explain the advantages of the rate table.

  • How can the rate table help you answer questions about popping popcorn and making servings?
  • how can you answer questions about the popcorn when the numbers are not on the table? For example, how do you find the number of cups of popcorn you can make from 30 ounces of popcorn kernels?

• Answers will vary.
  • Some students may extend the table. Some students may imagine making jumps on the table, like multiplying the amount for 10 ounces of kernels by 3. Some students will use a general expression, like for every 1 ounce of kernels, you get 4 cups of popcorn.

With each strategy that students discuss, have them justify why the strategy work. Then ask students to connect how the different strategy works. Then ask students to connect how the different strategies represent the same type of thinking.

  • How do you find how many ounces of popcorn kernels are needed to make 100 cups of popcorn?
  • How do the rte tables help you find unit rates?
  • How do the rate tables help you write algebraic expressions for the relationship between the variables?

• Some students may extend the table. Some students may imagine making jumps on the table, like multiplying the amount for 20 cups of popcorn by 5 to get 100 cups of popcorn. Some students will use a general expression, like the number of cups of popcorn divided by 4 tells how many ounces of kernels are needed.
  • Use the ratio reasoning in the tables to get oe of the quantities equal to 1.
  • The tables help you to see the repeated pattern that can be written as a rule.
